source.title: Causal evidence for the role of REM sleep theta rhythm in contextual memory consolidation

confidence_rationale: Causal optogenetic silencing of medial septum GABAergic neurons in mice attenuates theta rhythm during REM specifically (not during waking locomotion or NREM). REM-specific theta attenuation impairs object-place recognition and contextual fear memory; identical silencing outside REM epochs has no effect. This is the cleanest available causal dissociation of a REM-mode gate from a SWS-mode or waking gate, and it directly supports MECH-261's requirement that the REM write-gate is a functionally separable channel operating on a distinct carrier rhythm from SWS.
failure_signatures: Silencing MS-GABAergic neurons *outside* REM produced no memory impairment, demonstrating the mode specificity of the theta gate. A MECH-261 implementation in which the REM gate is structurally identical to the SWS gate (same carrier, same coupling geometry) cannot reproduce this temporal selectivity.

REM-specific theta attenuation erased memory formation rather than merely degrading it. This predicts that ablating MECH-261's REM-mode gate in REE should not simply reduce the REM pass's effect proportionally -- it should remove it, because the theta carrier is the enabling rhythm, not a modulator of an already-working channel.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Medial-septal GABAergic neurons generate the REM theta rhythm; optogenetically silencing these neurons selectively during REM attenuates theta and abolishes consolidation of hippocampus-dependent contextual memory, while identical silencing during non-REM states has no effect.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-261 treats each operating mode as a separable channel with its own gate vector. Boyce is the cleanest causal evidence that REM and non-REM gates are not just quantitatively different but functionally dissociable, with REM consolidation specifically requiring theta as the carrier rhythm. In REE this maps onto the SalienceCoordinator's REM-mode probability multiplying the write gate for downstream targets differently than the SWS-mode probability does, and it grounds the requirement that SD-017's REM pass must be gated on a theta-analog clock (not on the same SO-spindle-ripple geometry as the SWS pass).
mapping.mapping_caveat: Boyce demonstrate that REM theta is required for contextual memory consolidation; they do not directly show that theta routes writes to a specific cortical subdivision (the subdivision-selective evidence is the Maingret/Helfrich pair for SWS -- the equivalent REM study does not exist in this cohort). Treat this as the mode-separability evidence: the REM gate is not identical to the SWS gate. The within-REM target selection is an open question that the REE implementation currently addresses by picking a default REM write target (HC viability / residue replay) that the literature supports weakly rather than strongly.
mapping.source_context: Adult C57BL/6J mice; MS-GABAergic neurons targeted via ArchT in Vgat-Cre lines; closed-loop REM detection; object-place recognition and contextual fear conditioning as memory readouts.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality very high (Science, optogenetic causal, rigorous within-REM vs outside-REM control). Mapping fidelity 0.75 because Boyce isolate mode-separability of the theta gate but not target-selectivity of the REM write, and the target-selectivity question is the hardest part of MECH-261. Transfer risk 0.25: theta and MS-GABA homology between rodents and humans is well-established.
